sections.text: The Code of Criminal Procedure 1973 extends to the whole of India and applies to all offenses under the Indian Penal Code and other laws.
All offenses under the Indian Penal Code shall be investigated, inquired into, tried, and otherwise dealt with according to the provisions of this Code.
Offenses under the IPC are classified as cognizable and non-cognizable, bailable and non-bailable, and compoundable and non-compoundable.

title: Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices

sections.text: Criminal courts include: Supreme Court, High Courts, Courts of Session, Judicial Magistrates of First Class, Second Class, and Executive Magistrates.
The High Court establishes Courts of Session presided over by a Sessions Judge appointed by the High Court.
In every district, the Chief Judicial Magistrate is appointed who exercises supervision over other Judicial Magistrates.
Judicial Magistrates of the First Class are appointed in every district by the High Court.
Offenses punishable with death, imprisonment for life, or imprisonment exceeding 7 years shall be tried by a Court of Session.
Any offense not punishable with death, life imprisonment, or imprisonment exceeding 7 years may be tried by a Judicial Magistrate of the First Class.
A Judicial Magistrate of the Second Class may try offenses punishable with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine up to Rs. 5000.
A Court of Session may pass any sentence authorized by law, but any death sentence must be confirmed by the High Court.
A Chief Judicial Magistrate may pass any sentence except death, life imprisonment, or imprisonment exceeding 7 years.
A police officer may arrest without warrant any person concerned in a cognizable offense, or against whom a reasonable complaint has been made.
In cases where arrest is not necessary, the police officer shall issue a notice of appearance. If the person complies, no arrest shall be made.
In making an arrest, the police officer shall actually touch or confine the body of the person unless there is submission to custody by word or action.
A police officer may break open any door or window to enter and search any place if entry is otherwise impossible, after giving notice of purpose.
Every person arrested without warrant shall be informed of the full particulars of the offense for which he is arrested immediately.
A police officer making an arrest shall produce the arrested person before a magistrate without unnecessary delay, within 24 hours.
No person arrested shall be detained for more than 24 hours without being produced before a magistrate.
Arrest shall not be made unnecessarily and no more restraint than necessary shall be used.
Summons shall be in writing, signed by the presiding officer, and shall state the offense charged.
Every summons shall be served by a police officer or public servant by delivering a copy to the person summoned.
Where the person cannot be found, the summons may be served by leaving a copy with an adult family member.
Where there is no reasonable likelihood of personal service, the serving officer may affix a copy of the summons to the outer door of the residence.
Service by post is permissible in certain cases where the court deems it fit.
A warrant of arrest shall be signed by the presiding officer and shall bear the seal of the court.
Every warrant of arrest shall be directed to one or more police officers, requiring them to arrest the person named.
A warrant may be directed to any person for execution outside the local jurisdiction of the court.
If a person absconds to avoid warrant, the court may publish a proclamation requiring his appearance.
After proclamation, the court may order attachment of the absconder's property.

title: Processes to Compel Production of Things

sections.text: A court may issue a summons for the production of any document or other thing necessary for investigation or trial.
The court may summon a public servant to produce a public document in his custody.
A search warrant may be issued where the court has reason to believe a person will not produce a document or thing.
Search warrants may be issued for search of a place suspected to contain stolen property, forged documents, or evidence of an offense.
A magistrate may issue a search warrant for a person wrongfully confined.
When a search is conducted, the officer shall call upon independent witnesses and shall prepare a list of all things seized.

title: Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behavior

sections.text: A court may require a person convicted of certain offenses to execute a bond to keep the peace for a period not exceeding 3 years.
When a magistrate receives information that a person is likely to commit a breach of peace or disturb public tranquility, he may require a bond for keeping the peace.
Security for good behavior may be required from persons disseminating seditious matter or promoting class hatred.
Security may be required from persons suspected of concealing a design to commit a cognizable offense.
Security may be required from habitual offenders, robbers, thieves, and persons who are a danger to society.
A police officer may prevent any injury attempted to be caused to any public property.
A police officer may intervene to prevent the commission of cognizable offenses.
A police officer knowing of a design to commit a cognizable offense shall arrest the person without order from a magistrate.

title: Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquility

sections.text: A magistrate may issue orders in urgent cases of nuisance or apprehended danger, directing a person to abstain from a certain act or to take order with property in his possession.
A magistrate may make orders to prevent disputes concerning land or water likely to cause a breach of peace.

title: Maintenance of Wives, Children and Parents

sections.text: If a person having sufficient means neglects to maintain his wife, children, or parents who are unable to maintain themselves, a magistrate may order monthly maintenance of up to Rs. 5000.
Proceedings under Section 125 may be taken against any person in any district where he resides or where his wife resides.
An order under Section 125 may be altered or modified on proof of change in circumstances.
A magistrate may enforce payment of maintenance by issuing a warrant for levying the amount due.

title: Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate

sections.text: Every information relating to the commission of a cognizable offense given orally to a police officer shall be reduced to writing and signed by the informant. This is the First Information Report (FIR). A copy shall be given free of cost to the informant.
Information relating to non-cognizable offenses shall be entered in a diary and the police officer shall refer the informant to the magistrate.
A police officer may investigate any cognizable offense without the order of a magistrate.
If a police officer has reason to suspect the commission of a cognizable offense, he shall send a report to the magistrate and proceed to investigate.
A police officer may require attendance of witnesses acquainted with the facts for examination during investigation.
A police officer may examine orally any person supposed to be acquainted with the facts. The person is bound to answer truly except incriminating questions.
No statement made to a police officer during investigation shall be signed, and it may be used only for contradicting the witness in court.
A magistrate may record any confession or statement made during investigation. Confessions recorded under this section are admissible in evidence.

If investigation cannot be completed within 24 hours, the accused shall be produced before a magistrate who may authorize detention for up to 15 days. Total judicial custody may extend up to 90 days for serious offenses and 60 days for others.
If upon investigation there is no sufficient evidence, the police shall release the accused on bond.
If sufficient evidence is found, the police shall forward the accused to a magistrate for trial.
On completion of investigation, the police shall submit a final report (charge sheet) to the magistrate with all relevant documents.

title: Jurisdiction of Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials

sections.text: Every offense shall ordinarily be tried by a court within whose local jurisdiction it was committed.
Where the offense consists of several acts done in different local areas, the trial may be in any such area.
Where an act is done in one place and the consequence ensues in another, the trial may be in either place.
Where it is uncertain in which jurisdiction an offense was committed, the trial may be in any such area.

title: Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings

sections.text: A magistrate may take cognizance of an offense upon receiving a complaint, upon a police report, or upon information from any person.
A court shall not take cognizance of offenses against public justice or contempt of lawful authority except on complaint in writing by the concerned court.
For certain offenses against the State, cognizance may be taken only with prior sanction of the central or state government.
A magistrate taking cognizance of an offense on complaint shall examine the complainant and the witnesses on oath.
A magistrate taking cognizance of an offense on complaint shall examine the complainant upon oath and reduce the substance to writing.
The magistrate may postpone issuance of process and conduct an inquiry or direct investigation by police.
If the magistrate finds no sufficient ground for proceeding, he shall dismiss the complaint.
Every charge shall state the offense with which the accused is charged, giving the name and specific provision of law.
The charge shall contain particulars as to time, place, and person against whom the offense was committed.
For every distinct offense, there shall be a separate charge and each charge shall be tried separately.
When a person is charged with an offense and facts prove a minor offense, he may be convicted of the minor offense.
If the magistrate finds that the case is exclusively triable by the Court of Session, he shall commit the case to the Sessions Court.

title: Trial before a Court of Session

sections.text: When the accused is brought before the Court of Session, the prosecution shall open its case by describing the charge and stating the evidence.
If the judge finds no sufficient ground for proceeding, he shall discharge the accused.
If the judge finds grounds for presuming the accused committed the offense, he shall frame a charge in writing.
The prosecution shall produce its evidence after the charge is read and explained.
If after prosecution evidence the judge finds no evidence against the accused, he shall record an acquittal.
Where the accused is not acquitted, he shall enter his defense and produce evidence.
After hearing arguments, the judge shall give judgment. If convicted, the judge shall hear the accused on sentence.
If the magistrate finds the accused not guilty, he shall record an order of acquittal. If guilty, he shall pass sentence according to law.

title: Trial of Warrant Cases by Magistrates

sections.text: In warrant cases instituted on police report, the magistrate shall satisfy himself that the documents have been furnished to the accused.
If the magistrate considers the charge groundless, he shall discharge the accused.
If the magistrate finds reason to proceed, he shall frame a charge in writing against the accused.
The prosecution shall produce evidence after the charge is framed. The magistrate may issue summons to witnesses.
The accused may produce evidence in defense after the prosecution closes its case.
If the magistrate finds the accused not guilty, he shall acquit. If guilty, he shall convict and pass sentence.
If the complainant is absent, the magistrate may acquit the accused or adjourn the case.
If a complaint is found false or vexatious, the magistrate may order compensation to the accused.

title: Trial of Summons Cases by Magistrates

sections.text: In summons cases, the magistrate shall state the particulars of the offense to the accused and ask if he pleads guilty.
If the accused pleads guilty, the magistrate shall record the plea and convict him.
In petty cases, the magistrate may convict the accused on his plea of guilty without trial.
If the accused does not plead guilty, the magistrate shall hear the prosecution evidence and the accused's evidence.
If the magistrate finds the accused not guilty, he shall acquit. If guilty, he shall pass sentence.
In summons cases, the complainant may withdraw the complaint, and the magistrate shall acquit the accused.
In summons cases, the magistrate may stop proceedings and discharge the accused at any stage without recording an acquittal.
The following offenses may be tried summarily: offenses punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years, theft, receiving stolen property, criminal trespass, etc.
In summary trials, no sentence of imprisonment exceeding 3 months shall be passed.
The magistrate shall maintain a brief record of the substance of evidence and particulars of conviction.
A person arrested for a non-bailable offense shall be released on bail if the offense is bailable. The police officer shall release him on executing a bond.
A person accused of a non-bailable offense may be released on bail, except where there are reasonable grounds to believe guilt of an offense punishable with death or life imprisonment.
A High Court or Court of Session may grant bail to any person accused of any offense, including non-bailable offenses.
The amount of bail shall be fixed with due regard to the circumstances and shall not be excessive.
Before a person is released on bail, a bond for the sum fixed shall be executed.
A court may accept a deposit of money or valuable property in lieu of a bond.
If bail is forfeited, the bond and any surety amount shall be forfeited to the government.
No appeal shall lie from a judgment of acquittal unless the State government directs an appeal. The victim also has a right to appeal.
Appeals from convictions by the Court of Session lie to the High Court. Appeals from magistrate convictions lie to the Sessions Court.
No appeal lies where the accused pleads guilty and is convicted on such plea, except as to the extent or legality of the sentence.
No appeal lies in petty cases where the magistrate imposes a fine only of up to Rs. 100.
The State government may appeal against the inadequacy of sentence to the High Court.
The State government may appeal against an acquittal to the High Court.
After hearing an appeal, the appellate court may reverse, confirm, or modify the judgment, or order a new trial.
Pending appeal, the appellate court may suspend the sentence and release the appellant on bail.
A Sessions Judge may refer a question of law arising in a case to the High Court.
The High Court or Sessions Judge may call for and examine the records of any inferior criminal court to satisfy themselves on the correctness or legality of any finding, sentence or order.
The High Court may exercise revision powers suo motu or on application. No revision lies against an order of acquittal.
The power of revision shall not be exercised in favor of the convicted person unless an opportunity of being heard is given to the prosecution.

Nothing in this Code shall be deemed to limit or affect the inherent powers of the High Court to make such orders as may be necessary to give effect to any order under this Code, or to prevent abuse of the process of any court, or otherwise to secure the ends of justice.
